topic: Option 1: Relationships / Gender / Cognition and Development
subject: psychology

items.q: Which part of the psyche operates on the reality principle?
items.why: Students confuse ego (reality) with superego (morality). The ego mediates between id's demands and reality's constraints. The superego is the internalised moral conscience.

items.q: What does the dopamine hypothesis of schizophrenia state?
items.correct: Positive symptoms are linked to excess dopamine activity in mesolimbic pathways.
items.misconception: Schizophrenia is caused by too little dopamine.
items.random_wrong: Schizophrenia is caused by too much serotonin.
items.why: Students reverse the direction. The original hypothesis was HYPERdopaminergia. The revised version notes hypoactivity in prefrontal areas for negative symptoms, but excess in mesolimbic for positive.

items.correct: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to INCREASE a behaviour.
items.misconception: Punishing a behaviour to decrease it.
items.random_wrong: Giving a reward to decrease behaviour.
items.why: Students confuse negative reinforcement with punishment. Both involve something unpleasant, but reinforcement (positive or negative) always INCREASES behaviour; punishment decreases it.

items.q: The SRY gene that triggers testis development sits on which chromosome?
items.why: It's Y. “13” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Kohlberg's final stage of gender development is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Gender constancy. “Gender identity” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: At roughly what age does Kohlberg say gender constancy is reached?
items.why: Exact answer: 7 years. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
